Items.Title: 自定义 React Hooks 的 5 个基本技巧 | 5 Essential Tips for Custom React Hooks
Items.Description:
那么，您是否经常担心您当前正在编写的钩子永远不会在您的整个应用程序中重复使用，除非在一个单独的实例中，或者该钩子甚至应该处理什么，您应该编写该钩子中的内容一个依赖于另一个钩子的钩子，或者两者都应该只是一个巨型钩子，它返回一个易于解构的对象，允许您或您的团队成员访问钩子状态或方法。 哦，然后有所有那些超级有用的钩子库，只需一个 wbe 搜索即可，您理想的钩子只需快速复制粘贴即可，您应该使用它们还是应该自己编写？ 好吧，我在这里告诉你应该编写自己的，我将给你 5 个技巧来帮助你编写更好的自定义钩子。

在编写自定义钩子时，您应该问自己的第一件事是“这个钩子会在我的应用程序的多个地方重复使用吗？”，甚至它有可能在多个地方重复使用。 答案并不像你想象的那么简单。 例如，假设我们有一个名为useAuth的自定义钩子，它处理您的身份验证逻辑，例如登录用户以注销用户，检索有关用户的一些信息并将其存储在易于访问的位置，即使用户关闭浏览器等。您的首先想到的可能是用户只需要登录和注销一次，并且用户信息可以存储在应用程序状态中，因此显然这不是挂钩的理想候选者。

但是，您真的希望供应商将您的身份验证锁定到您当前使用的任何应用程序状态库。 您真的希望为您编写的每个应用程序一遍又一遍地重写您的身份验证逻辑吗？ 如果该库的维护者决定不再维护他们的代码库，或者更糟糕的是更改了破坏您的身份验证逻辑的代码库的核心部分，该怎么办。 您真的想在您的应用程序安全方面信任其他人吗？ 不，你没有。

这就是为什么useAuth钩子是自定义钩子的理想选择。 但是您可能想知道好吧，但是登录和注销仍然只能由用户使用一次，所以如果它不能重用，那有什么意义呢。 现在确定该部分可能不是完全可重用的，但是检索用户信息并将其存储在易于访问的地方的部分是可重用的。 因此，您可以拥有一个处理登录和注销的useAuth钩子和一个处理用户信息检索并将其存储在易于访问的地方的useUser钩子。 此外，您可以在useAuth钩子中调用useUser钩子，这样您就可以通过简单地从useAuth钩子返回用户信息来轻松访问useAuth钩子中的用户信息

例子：

 // useAuth.ts import { useUser } from " ./useUser " ; // import the useUser hook export const useAuth = () => { const { user , setUser , clearUser } = useUser (); const login = async ( email : string , password : string ) => { // login logic goes here ie via react-query or axios setUser ( user ); }; const logout = async () => { // logout logic goes here ie via react-query or axios clearUser (); // if you're storing your user info in local storage this method should delete it }; // return the user and the login and logout methods return { user , login , logout }; };

我看到人们犯的第二个错误是将他们的自定义钩子过度设计成这些大型钩子，这些钩子可以同时处理太多的功能。 理想情况下，您应该在这里遵循“做一件事并做好”的原则。 这意味着如果您想编写一个将日期转换为人类可读字符串的钩子，那么您也不应该尝试填充处理时区逻辑或任何此类内容的功能。 你最好有：

  • 处理将日期转换为人类可读字符串的useRelativeDates钩子
  • 记住简单是关键，但请不要过度。 不需要有 5 行长的钩子，除了返回一个值什么都不做。 除非你有一个非常具体的用例。

    一切都在适度蚱蜢。

    说真的，我知道我已经在我的另一篇博文中讨论了整个事情，你的组件应该有多精细

    但实际上，测量两次切割一次。 在编写单行代码之前，为我们的应用程序及其各个部分计划一个粗略的想法或概念要容易得多。 这自然也伴随着经验，所以如果你是一个相当有经验的开发人员，你可能不需要为你编写的每个钩子或组件制定计划阶段。 但我认为自己相当有经验，我可以完全肯定地告诉你，每当我在编写代码之前花时间计划和搭建我的代码时，我最终会得到更清晰和可维护的代码，并且我会发现很多错误或奇怪的思路，否则我会错过或会在干扰某些曲调时盲目实施。

    我知道在你最喜欢的搜索引擎中输入自定义钩子 useWord 是非常诱人的，找到与你的用例最接近的那个，复制粘贴它并完成它。 但这不是正确的方法。 首先，您正在剥夺自己的学习经验，其次，您永远无法 100% 确定您找到的钩子是以完全适合您的用例的方式编写的，并且您真的不想调整您的用例以适应钩。 那将是倒退。

    相反，请查找其他人创建的钩子，通读它，研究它，看看哪些部分适合您的用例，哪些不适合。 使用合适的部分并扔掉其余部分，添加一些你自己的东西，然后你就拥有了一个你可以引以为豪的自定义钩子，它是真正属于你自己的，应该完美地适合你的特定用例。

    我们已经在第一个示例中介绍了这一点，而不是编写一个处理许多功能的大型钩子，而是考虑编写多个非常具体的较小钩子， use后的单词应该始终完美地描述钩子的作用，例如useAuth ， useUser , useLocalStorage , useAxios , useNewsApi等。

    我们已经介绍过，您可以在另一个钩子中使用一个钩子，然后在一个易于解构的对象中返回两个钩子的值。

    如果它与 react 和 react hooks 的整个哲学联系在一起，那么采用这种方法并没有错。 所以不要害怕在另一个钩子里面使用一个钩子。

    结论

    我希望这篇文章能帮助你理解自定义钩子的重要性以及如何编写它们。 我已经编写自定义钩子有一段时间了，我可以完全肯定地告诉你，它们是你的 react 开发工具带中最强大的工具之一。 它们本质上允许我们将组件的功能抽象为可重用的功能，我们可以在应用程序的多个位置使用这些功能。 所有这一切都无需求助于任何讨厌的OOP 。 🤮 🤮



原文:

So how often do you fret that the hook you are currently writing is never going to be re-used in your entire application except in one single lonely instance, or how about what should that hook even handle, what goes in that hook should you write a hook that depends on another hook or should both of those just be one mega hook that return an easy to destructure object that allows you or your team mates to access the hooks state or methods. Oh and then there's all those super useful hook libraries that are just one wbe search away where your ideal hook is just a quick copy paste away, should you use those or should you write your own? Well, I'm here to tell you that you should write your own, and I'm going to give you 5 tips to help you write better custom hooks.

1. Reusability is king 👑

The first thing you should ask yourself when writing a custom hook is "Will this hook be re-used in multiple places in my application?", or even does it have the potential to be re-used in multiple places. The answer is not as easy as you might think. Let's for example say that we have a custom hook named useAuth which handles your authentication logic such as logging the user in logging the user out, retrieving some information about the user and storing it somewhere easily accesible even if the user closes their browser etc. Your first thought might be well the user only ever needs to log in and log out once and the user info can just be stored in the application state, so clearly this is not an ideal candidate for a hook.

However do you really want to vendor lock your auth to whatever application state library you're currently using. Do you really want to have to rewrite your auth logic over and over and over again for every single application you write? How about if the maintainer of that library decides to no longer maintain their codebase or even worse changes a core part of their codebase that breaks your auth logic. Do you really wanna trust someone else with your applications security? No, you don't.

Which is why a useAuth hook is an ideal candidate for a custom hook. But you may be wondering okay but the login and logout will still only ever be used once by the user so what's the point if it's not reusable. Now sure that part may not be all that reusable, however the part that retrieves the user info and stores it somewhere easily accessible is reusable. So you can have a useAuth hook that handles the login and logout and a useUser hook that handles the retrieval of the user info and storing it somewhere easily accessible. Furthermore you can call the useUser hook inside of the useAuth hook so that you can easily access the user info in the useAuth hook by simply returning the user info from the useAuth hook

Example: 

// useAuth.ts
import { useUser } from "./useUser"; // import the useUser hook export const useAuth = () => { const { user, setUser, clearUser } = useUser(); const login = async (email: string, password: string) => { // login logic goes here i.e via react-query or axios setUser(user); }; const logout = async () => { // logout logic goes here i.e via react-query or axios clearUser(); // if you're storing your user info in local storage this method should delete it }; // return the user and the login and logout methods return { user, login, logout };
};

2. Don't overcomplicate your hooks

The second mistake I see people make is over engineer their custom hooks into these mega hooks that tackle too much functionallity at once. Optimally you'd want to follow the do one thing and do it well principle here. Meaning if you wanna write a hook that transforms your dates into nice human readable strings then you should not also try and cram in functionality that handles timezone logic or any of that stuff. You'd optimally have:

  • useRelativeDates hook that handles transforming dates into human readable strings
  • useTimezone hook that handles timezone logic, whatever it may be

Remember simplicity is key, but don't overdo it please. No need to have hooks that are 5 lines long and do nothing but return a value. Unless you have a very specific use case for that.

Everything in moderation grasshopper.

3. Plan out your hooks

Seriously I know I've gone into this whole thing in my other blog post How Granular Should your Components be

But really, measure twice cut once. It's much easier to plan our a rough idea or concept of what your application and it's individual parts before you write a single line of code. This naturally comes with experience as well so if you're a fairly experienced developer maybe you don't need a planning phase for every single hook or component you write. But I'd consider myself fairly experienced and I can tell you with full certainty whenever I do take my time to plan out and scaffold my code before I write it I end up with much cleaner and maintainable code and I catch a lot of mistakes or weird trains of thought that I would have otherwise missed or would have mindlesly implemented while jamming out to some tunes.

4. Do search up other peoples hooks, don't copy paste

I know it's super tempting to just type in custom hook useWord into your favorite search engine, find the one that matches your use case the closest, copy paste it and be done with it. But that's not the right way to go about it. Firstly you're robbing yourself of a learning experience, secondly you can never be 100% sure the hook you've found is written in a way that suits your use case perfectly and you REALLY do not want to adjust your use case to fit the hook. That would be backwards.

Instead do look up a hook that somebody else has created, read it through, study it and see which parts fit your use case and which do not. Use the parts that do fit and throw away the rest, add some of your own stuff to it and then you've got yourself a custom hook that you can be proud of and is truly your own and should fit your specific use case perfectly.

5. Don't be afraid to write a hook that depends on another hook

We already covered this in our first example, instead of writing a big mega hook that tackles a lot of functionality consider writing multiple smaller hooks that are very specific the word that comes after use should always perfectly describe what the hook does, e.g useAuth, useUser, useLocalStorage, useAxios, useNewsApi etc.

We've already covered that you can use one hook inside of another and then return the values of both hooks in an easily deconstructable object.

There is nothing wrong with taking this approach if anything it ties into the whole philosophy of react and react hooks. React is all about composition and reusability and so are react hooks. So don't be afraid to use one hook inside of another.

Conclusion

I hope this post has helped you understand the importance of custom hooks and how to write them. I've been writing custom hooks for a while now and I can tell you with full certainty that they are one of the most powerful tools in your react developer toolbelt. They essentially allow us to abstract functionality away from our components into reusable functions that we can use in multiple places in our application. All of this without having to resort to any nasty OOP. 🤮

原文:

In this blog post we're going to look at the top tech companies and startups in Silicon Valley by amount of money raised. We'll then slice and dice to see the top startups for each funding round stage like Series A, Series B etc. We're not going to include companies that have been acquired by other companies or companies that have satellite offices in the Silicon Valley area. The companies on these lists all have their headquarters in Silicon Valley.

It's worth noting that "Silicon Valley" isn't a real geographic place. Despite literally the most heavily traffic'd post on Employbl being "map of silicon valley companies" Silicon Valley is only a geographic term in the loosest sense. The term originates from the microchip days when defense spending poured into the area to make many in San Francisco very wealthy. This term originated before the internet but especially took off in the 90s. For the purposes of this post we're going to define Silicon Valley as existing in the following cities: Menlo Park, Mountain View, Palo Alto, Cupertino, Redwood City, San Bruno, San Jose, San Mateo, Santa Clara and Sunnyvale. Please note this completely excludes San Francisco. If you're interested in learning about the history of Silicon Valley, both the place and the term I recommend reading Margaret O'Mara's book called The Code. Without further ado let's look at the full list of top startups headquartered in Silicon Valley..

Largest Tech Companies in Silicon Valley

To start off let's look at the largest companies that are headquartered in Silicon Valley by estimated number of employees. To learn more about a company you can click on their name to navigate through to their Employbl profile where we'll showcase more info about the business like the founders, funding round and in some cases active job listings. Shout out to Diffbot for scraping the whole public internet to find data about these organizations, including employee count, funding info and even data about the technologies these companies use.

There are 753 companies based in Silicon Valley that are stored in the Employbl database. Log in to explore all the data.

These are some of the biggest "can't miss" companies that are headquartered in Silicon Valley. In many ways they're the companies the valley is most known for, especially the top ten. Let's move on to look at top startups in Silicon Valley.

Series A startups in Silicon Valley

To learn what firms or individuals participated in the company's Seed, Pre Seed or Series A rounds click through to each startup's Employbl profile. For this list we're going to order by companies that have raised the most money at the top.

Series B companies headquartered in Silicon Valley

I feel like "Series B" stage companies aren't really startups anymore. The institutional finance is in. There will be leadership changes. Things will get "more legit". It still can definitely be a tech company but personally I think of startups as Series A companies and before. It's all semantics but whatever that's my unsolicited two cents. 原文:

Fala, Dev!

Estamos de volta com mais uma edição da EZ.pills 💊, uma newsletter em cápsulas de conteúdos para devs.

Na primeira vez que abriu um terminal, sentiu medo de bagunçar seu computador ou vontade de aprender mais sobre? 🤔

Bradesco conduz inovação no mercado com sandbox privado

O Bradesco criou um sandbox privado para o desenvolvimento das APIs do Open Finance, unindo tecnologia e parceria para avançar na inovação tecnológica. A plataforma adotada foi a Motive, da startup OOLYS e é usada em vários bancos globais na Europa, EUA e Austrália como APIs com padrões internacionais. A notícia pode ser lida no Convergência Digital🔗.

Pesquisa mostra como maus hábitos de desenvolvimento continuam - e que o WebAssembly é over hyped

O HTTP Archive publicou um relatório denominado Web Almanac baseado em uma pesquisa em 8 milhões de websites. Os principais insights foram de que: o WebAssembly não é usado com frequência e demonstra "contração" ao invés de crescimento; hábitos como formatos de imagens velhos e ineficientes ou sites dependendo de muitos scripts de terceiros continuam sendo muito praticados. A notícia pode ser lida no Dev Class🔗.

CTO da Microsoft Azure diz que está na hora da "grande migração" do C e C# para o Rust

Mark Russinovich indica que cenários que não necessitam de um garbage collector devem adotar o Rust como principal linguagem para projetos, sendo papel da indústria declarar as linguagens C e C# como defasadas. O comentário vem logo após a notícia da implementação de Rust no kernel Linux, o projeto C mais famoso da história. A notícia pode ser lida no The Register🔗.

Heroku cria planos low cost após decisão polêmica

Após anunciar o fim dos planos gratuitos, a Heroku criou planos low cost para mitigar o baque no relacionamento com desenvolvedores. Além de terem custos baixos por horas computadas que irão "dormir" (se inativar) após 30 minutos sem tráfego, há "mini-planos" exclusivos para Postgres e Redis. A notícia pode ser lida no Dev Class🔗.

"Instagram sem anúncios" é banido da App Store

OG App é uma plataforma recentemente lançada que permite a navegação no Instagram sem anúncios ao usuário. Logo após a Meta anunciar que tomou medidas contra o app, a Apple decidiu removê-lo da App Store. Desenvolvedores do projeto relatam que fizeram uma engenharia reversa da API e todos as contas pessoas de funcionários do projeto foram permanentemente banidas do Facebook e Instagram. A notícia pode ser lida no Olhar Digital🔗.

📚 Dica de Conteúdo

Tendências do desenvolvimento web para 2023

Separamos um artigo da Yellow Systems com 14 tendências no desenvolvimento de aplicações para web. Tecnologias e features que deslancharam durante o ano prometem ser um sucesso no ano que vem. Acesse o link🔗 para saber mais!

E assim nós finalizamos mais uma edição da sua dose semanal de pílulas de conteúdo! 💙

Items.Title: 编程：先计划，后编码 | Programming: Plan First, Then Code
Items.Description:
许多新手程序员试图直接开始编写代码（用编程语言）作为第一步。 但是，编写代码实际上是该过程中的一个较晚的步骤。 一个好的程序员会先计划后写，可能会在这个过程中将一个大的编程任务分解成几个较小的任务。 即使被警告要先计划，再编码，许多编程学生还是忽略了这个建议——毕竟，为什么在你必须做的所有工作时间紧迫的情况下“浪费”30 分钟的计划。 然而，这种权衡带来了一种虚假的经济性——30 分钟的计划可以节省数小时试图使代码正常工作的时间。 精心计划的代码不仅更可能是正确的（或至少更接近正确），而且更容易理解——从而修复。

为了在写作之前更好地理解规划的重要性，可以想象一下建造房屋或摩天大楼的类比。 如果您的任务是建造一座摩天大楼，您是否会破土动工并立即开始建造，并在进行时弄清楚建筑物的设计方式？ 希望不会。 相反，您（或建筑师）将首先为建筑物设计蓝图。 这些蓝图会被交互地细化，直到它们满足每个人的规格——它们必须满足建筑物所有者的要求，并且可以合理地建造。 蓝图一旦完成，必须得到当地政府的批准。 实际建设只有在计划完全完成后才开始。 编程应该以类似的方式进行——首先提出一个完整的计划（算法），然后构建（在代码中实现）。

我们说过，编程的核心是弄清楚如何解决一类问题——而不仅仅是一个特定的问题。 这里的区别最好用一个例子来解释。 考虑确定一个特定数字（例如，7）是否为素数的任务。 有了足够的数学知识（即素数的定义和除法规则），就可以解决这个问题——确定 7 实际上是素数。 但是，编程问题通常着眼于更一般的问题类别。 我们通常不会编写一个程序来确定 7 是否为素数，而是编写一个程序，给定一个数字 N，确定 N 是否为素数。 一旦我们有了针对这类一般问题的算法，我们就可以让计算机为我们解决问题的任何特定实例。

当我们检查一类问题时，我们有参数告诉我们正在解决该类中的哪个特定问题。 在前面的示例中，问题类别由 N 参数化——我们要测试素数的数字。 为了开发此类问题的算法，我们必须考虑参数的所有可能的合法值。 正如我们稍后将看到的，编程语言允许我们限制参数可以表示的信息类型，以将合法值限制为在问题上下文中有意义的值。 对于素数测试，我们希望参数 N 受到限制，使其只能容纳整数。 检查字母、单词或文件是否为质数是没有任何意义的。
要编写一个取任意数 N 并确定 N 是否为素数的程序，我们必须首先弄清楚这类问题的算法。 正如我们之前所说，如果我们盲目地编写代码来解决问题，我们最终会陷入混乱——就像没有计划地建造摩天大楼一样。 为一类问题提出合适的算法是一项具有挑战性的任务，通常需要大量的工作和思考。

原文:

Many novice programmers attempt to dive right into writing the code (in the programming language) as the first step. However, writing the code is actually a much later step in the process. A good programmer will plan first and write second, possibly breaking down a large programming task into several smaller tasks in the process. Even when cautioned to plan first and code second, many programming students ignore the advice—after all, why “waste” 30 minutes planning when you are time-crunched from all the work you have to do. This trade-off, however, presents a false economy—30 minutes planning could save hours of trying to make the code work properly. Well planned code is not only more likely to be correct (or at least closer to correct), but is also easier to understand—and thus fix.

To try to better understand the importance of planning before you write, imagine an analogy to building a house or sky scraper. If you were tasked with building a sky scraper, would you break ground and start building right away, figuring out how the building is designed as you go? Hopefully not. Instead, you (or an architect) would design blueprints for the building first. These blueprints would be interactively refined until they meet everyone’s specifications—they must meet the requirements of the building’s owner, as well as be possible to build reasonably. Once the blueprints are completed, they must be approved by the local government. Actual construction only begins once the plans are fully completed. Programming should be done in a similar manner— come up with a complete plan (algorithm) first and build (implement in code) second.

We said that the heart of programming is to figure out how to solve a class of problems—not just one particular problem. The distinction here is best explained by an example. Consider the task of figuring out if a particular number (e.g., 7) is prime. With sufficient knowledge of math (i.e., the definition of a prime number and the rules of division), one can solve this problem—determining that 7 is in fact prime. However, a programming problem typically looks at a more general class of problems. We would typically not write a program to determine if 7 is prime, but rather a program which, given a number N, determines if N is prime. Once we have an algorithm for this general class of problems, we can have the computer solve any particular instance of the problem for us.

When we examine a class of problems, we have parameters which tell us which particular problem in the class we are solving. In the previous example, the class of problems is parameterized by N—the number we want to test for primality. To develop an algorithm for this class of problems, we must account for all possible legal values of the parameters. As we will see later, programming languages let us restrict what type of information a parameter can represent, to limit the legal values to those which make sense in the context of the problem. For primality testing, we would want our parameter N to be restricted such that it can only hold integer numbers. It would not make any sense to check if letters, words, or files are prime.
To write a program which takes any number N and determines if N is prime, we must first figure out the algorithm for this class of problems. As we said before, if we attack the problem by blindly writing code, we will end up with a mess—much like constructing a sky scraper with no plan. Coming up with the appropriate algorithm for a class of problems is a challenging task, and typically requires significant work and thought.

Roshan Shambharkar - Sep 29
